title: AML Agents Briefing — Thursday, 02 July 2026
script:
ALEX: Welcome to AML Agents Briefing. It's Wednesday the second of July 2026. I'm Alex, joined as always by Jordan. This is your audio overview of today's most material AML and financial crime developments across Asia-Pacific. For the underlying detail, full obligation register, and horizon scanning, head to amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

JORDAN: Big day, Alex. We've got AI in retail banking, a significant enforcement action out of Australia, and some forward-looking items that compliance teams really shouldn't push to next week.

ALEX: Let's get into it. The lead story today sits at the intersection of AI deployment and customer due diligence obligations. OCBC Bank has rolled out AI-powered financial advisor avatars inside its retail mobile application. On the surface this looks like a product story, but for MLROs it raises immediate questions under the Monetary Authority of Singapore's Notice 626 on prevention of money laundering and countering the financing of terrorism.

JORDAN: Right, and the core issue here is whether the AI advisor interaction constitutes a customer touchpoint that triggers or refreshes a CDD obligation. If the avatar is gathering financial information, making recommendations, or facilitating product onboarding, that workflow needs to sit inside your CDD governance, not outside it.

ALEX: Exactly. MAS Notice 626 requires financial institutions to conduct ongoing CDD, and any automated channel that touches customer financial data or behaviour is in scope for that obligation. The question compliance officers need to answer is whether the avatar's outputs feed into transaction monitoring or risk-rating systems, and if not, why not.

JORDAN: So the action here is fairly clear. If you're the MLRO or head of financial crime compliance at a Singapore-licensed retail bank, pull your AI governance framework this week and check whether new AI-facing customer touchpoints are explicitly captured in your CDD and ongoing monitoring procedures. If they aren't, that's a gap you want to close before your next MAS inspection, not during it.

ALEX: Moving on, and staying with the theme of AI in financial services, there's a cross-border dimension worth flagging. US neobank SoFi has acquired Composer, a Toronto-based AI-driven retail investing platform. The compliance read-across for APAC is indirect but real. Structures like this, where an AI investment capability is bolted onto a licensed entity through acquisition, tend to move faster than the compliance integration that needs to follow them.

JORDAN: And what regulators across this region will look for is whether the acquired entity's customer base, its transaction flows, and its risk ratings are properly absorbed into the acquirer's AML programme. For heads of financial crime at digital banks or fintechs considering similar acquisitions, the lesson is to run an AML due diligence workstream in parallel with commercial due diligence, not after signing.

ALEX: Good point. Now, shifting our focus to enforcement, and this is the item I'd encourage every listener to act on this week. AUSTRAC has levied a civil penalty of forty-seven million Australian dollars against a major registered Digital Currency Exchange for systemic failures across transaction monitoring, mule-account detection, and Suspicious Matter Report timeliness. This caps a twelve-month investigation and is one of the largest penalties AUSTRAC has issued against a Digital Currency Exchange.

JORDAN: The three failure categories named in that enforcement are not niche issues. Transaction monitoring gaps, failure to identify mule accounts, and late SMR lodgement are the bread and butter of any AML programme. If those three are failing at a registered exchange, the regulator is going to look hard at every other registered entity in the same sector.

ALEX: So what should listeners do with this?

JORDAN: If you're the MLRO or AML compliance officer at an AUSTRAC-registered entity, particularly a Digital Currency Exchange or a remittance provider, I'd do three things before the end of this week. First, pull your last six months of SMR lodgements and check the average time from detection to submission. AUSTRAC expects prompt reporting, and delays are a direct enforcement trigger under Part 3 of the AML and CTF Act. Second, run a spot-check on your mule-account detection rules. The Bank Negara Malaysia typology bulletin from April flags rapid post-onboarding velocity spikes and inbound flows from multiple unrelated retail senders as key indicators, and those patterns translate directly into an Australian context. Third, if you haven't reviewed your transaction monitoring coverage since your last independent audit, raise that with your risk committee now, not at the next scheduled review.

ALEX: What to watch as we close out. AUSTRAC has published detailed Tranche 2 industry guidance for legal practitioners, covering customer engagement triggers, source-of-wealth diligence in conveyancing transactions, and the legal professional privilege carve-outs that have been a sticking point for the profession. If your institution has exposure to law firm clients or trust accounts, this guidance shapes what your enhanced due diligence procedures for that segment need to look like.

JORDAN: And on the obligations front, Hong Kong-licensed institutions should note the HKMA AML and CFT Guideline section eleven self-assessment return. That covers the 2024 reporting period and requires Internal Audit to independently validate the submission before it goes in. If your Internal Audit team hasn't been briefed on the scope, now is the time to do that.

JORDAN: That's your briefing for Wednesday the second of July. Head to amlagents dot streamlit dot app for the full detail on every item we've covered today, plus the complete obligation register and horizon-scanning feed.

ALEX: Thanks for listening. We'll be back tomorrow. Stay sharp.
